coredata.dc:title: A specific nanoprobe for cysteine based on nitrogen-rich fluorescent quantum dots combined with Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Tingting
Zou, Wu
Gong, Fuchun
Xia, Jiaoyun
Chen, Can
Chen, Xuejiao
coredata.dc:description: As a new member of the carbon quantum-dot family, fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NRQDs) were prepared by a mixed solvothermal method using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants. These NRQDs are rich in nitrogen up to 40.2%, which are endowed with high fluorescence quantum yield, good photostability, water-solubility and favourable biocompatibility. We further explored the use of NRQDs combined with Cu2+ as a nanoprobe for sensing fluorescently of cysteine (Cys) in complex biological samples. In this sensing system, the fluorescence is significantly quenched via energy transfer from NRQDs to Cu2+ for the coordination of amino-containing groups with Cu2+. The strong affinity between Cu 2+ and Cys leads to the formation of Cu2+-Cys complexes and cause the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of NRQDs, thus the fluorescence of NRQDs recover. This nanoprobe allows analysis of Cys by modulating the switch of the fluorescence of NRQDs with a detection limit of 5.3nM. As expected, the proposed NRQDs-Cu2+complex-based nanoprobes were successfully applied for the determination of Cys in human serum and plasma samples with recoveries ranging from 97.2% to 105.7%. The probe ensemble was also successfully applied to imaging of Cys in living cells with satisfactory results, which shows strong potential for clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-rich quantum dots
Synthesis
Nanoprobes
Cysteine
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of a fluorescent nanoprobe for Cys combined NRQDs with Cu2+
Detection of Cys with the NRQDs-Cu2+-based nanoprobe
Intracellular Cys fluorescence imaging in A549 cells
Synthesis and characterizations of the NRQDs
Construction of the nanoprobes and sensing principle for Cys detection
Optimization of the specific nanoprobes for Cys
Detection of Cys in human serum and plasma
Confocal microscopic imaging of Cys in A549 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs at different magnifications (a) 50nm, (b) 5nm. Inset shows the corresponding NRQDs size distribution histogram.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared NRQDs. (b) N1s spectra of the prepared NRQDs. IR spectra(c) and raman spectrum(d) of the prepared NRQDs.

(a) Selectivity of the NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes for Cys over the other amino acid species. (b)Response profiles of NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes by Cys in the presence of different amino acid species. The concentrations: Cys, 0. 5 Μm/L; the others, 5.0 Μm/L; NRQDs-Cu2+ concentration was 0.08mg/mL in PBS(20mmol, pH7.4). The incubation temperature was at room temperature, and incubation time was 20min. The excitation and emission is at 400 and 472nm, respectively. The slit widths of emission and excitation were 5nm.
Fluorescence spectra towards Cys with different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 9 and 10.0μM/L). Inset is the linear plot from 0.15 to 10.0μM/L.

Confocal microscopic images of A549 cells incubated with NRQDs and NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes. (A), (E): The bright field images of A549 cells; (B): The A549 cells were incubated in a 0.5μmol/L NRQDs solution for 1h after pretreatment with 1.0mmol/L NMM; (C): The A549 cells were incubated in the same NRQDs solution without pretreatment; (F): The A549 cells were incubated in a 0.5μmol/L NRQDs-Cu2+ solution for 1h after pretreatment with 1.0mmol/L NMM; (G): The A549 cells were incubated in the same NRQDs-Cu2+ solution for 1h without pretreatment.

(a) Synthesis strategy of the fluorescent NRQDs using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants by a mixed solvothermal method; (b) Sensing principle of the NRQDs-Cu2+ complex-based nanoprobes for Cys.

head.ce:title: A specific nanoprobe for cysteine based on nitrogen-rich fluorescent quantum dots combined with Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Biologic Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410004, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Biologic Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new member of the carbon quantum-dot family, fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NRQDs) were prepared by a mixed solvothermal method using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants. These NRQDs are rich in nitrogen up to 40.2%, which are endowed with high fluorescence quantum yield, good photostability, water-solubility and favourable biocompatibility. We further explored the use of NRQDs combined with Cu2+ as a nanoprobe for sensing fluorescently of cysteine (Cys) in complex biological samples. In this sensing system, the fluorescence is significantly quenched via energy transfer from NRQDs to Cu2+ for the coordination of amino-containing groups with Cu2+. The strong affinity between Cu 2+ and Cys leads to the formation of Cu2+-Cys complexes and cause the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of NRQDs, thus the fluorescence of NRQDs recover. This nanoprobe allows analysis of Cys by modulating the switch of the fluorescence of NRQDs with a detection limit of 5.3nM. As expected, the proposed NRQDs-Cu2+complex-based nanoprobes were successfully applied for the determination of Cys in human serum and plasma samples with recoveries ranging from 97.2% to 105.7%. The probe ensemble was also successfully applied to imaging of Cys in living cells with satisfactory results, which shows strong potential for clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) including quantum dots have attracted considerable attention owing to their outstanding chemical selectivity, optical and electronic properties (Sarkar et al., 2015; Arul et al., 2017). Among the nanostructures, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged and attracted growing interest in analytical and bioanalytical chemistry in recent years (Atchudan et al., 2016; Borse et al., 2017). Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) that usually contain heavy metal, for example, Cd2+, the good photostability, high biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity make CQDs good alternatives to SQDs for drug delivery to biological and biomedical imaging in vitro and in vivo (Babakhanian. et al., 2015; Boyaci. et al., 2015). However, the CQDs, as a new type of fluorescent probe, did not show obvious advantages over small molecular dye or other nanostructure-based probes in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, mainly due to the fact that the conventional CQDs did not show satisfied fluorescence activities, leading to low detection sensitivities, or the CQDs were not well functionalized, resulting in bad selectivities (Caputo et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2013). Evidently, the development of specially functionalized CQDs-based probes with relatively high quantum yield, water solubility and favourable biocompatibility has become very important for the analytical applications.

Modification with coating materials may produce strong fluorescent CQDs–based probes with controllable biocompatibility and stability. Up until now, many methods have been developed for the modification of CQDs are mainly focusing on the optoelectronic devices and bioimaging (Ooi et al., 2007; Qing et al., 2014; Qian et al., 2015). CQDs have been endowed with molecular recognition ability through surface functionalization and applied in chemical and biologic sensing. Various coating materials such as polyamine, N-(2-aminoethyl)-N,N,N′-tris(pyridine-2-ylmethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine, polyamide, ethylenediamine and polyethyleneimine are frequently used to modulate the chemical, optical and electronic properties of CQDs, which make the modified CQDs-based probes sensitive and selective to detection and imaging the metal ions, for example, Cu2+ in live cells (Qu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2017; Sarkar et al., 2014).

Doping with heteroatoms is another good strategy to tune the intrinsic properties of CQDs (Gao et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2016a, 2016b). As far as doping elements are concerned, electron-rich N atom is the most frequently used one to obtain the exceptional properties of CQDs (Kang et al., 2017; Weerapana et al., 2010a, 2010b). it has been shown that the modified CQDs with N could drastically increase quantum yield and give more active sites due to the electron-withdrawing ability of nitrogen atoms (Li et al., 2012; Niu et al., 2016; Stachniuk et al., 2016). To incorporate nitrogen groups into CQDs, several approaches have been developed, such as treatment of carbon materials with ammonia at high temperatures (Przepiorsi et al., 2004), carbonization of nitrogen containing compounds at high temperature (NCCs)(Jurgens et al., 2003), liquid phase polymerization of NCCs (Lee et al., 2009) or chemical vapor deposition with NCCs as a N source (Jin et al., 2011). However, all the above-mentioned methods suffer from a common drawback is that most of the nitrogen atoms were introduced into nanostructures under harsh conditions with relatively less content of element nitrogen (Hou et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2009), which may limit their wide applications. Thus, developing simple and low-cost methods for the production of CQDs material with relatively abundant content of element nitrogen is highly desired.

Recently, nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NRQDs), as a new member of the carbon quantum-dot family were prepared by refluxing in methanol, which showed distinct and unique optical properties (Chen et al., 2014). Compared to popular CQDs modified with N, these NRQDs contain a higher percentage of nitrogen content and can effectively tune their chemical selectivity and optical properties. Preliminary application studies on biocompatible staining, determination of natural drug, and detection of toxic cation of NRQDs were also carried out. These results demonstrated that the NRQDs exhibit excellent photoluminescence, high photostability, high quantum yield, water solubility and favourable biocompatibility, which is extremely important for their practical applications (Wu et al., 2016a, 2016b; Carrillo-Carrio´n et al., 2009; Tang et al., 2017; Rani and John, 2016).

As one of the most important small biothiols, cysteine (Cys) plays versatile roles in a variety of physiological processes such as biocatalysis, providing resistance to the body against harmful effects, post-translation modifying, stabilizing protein structures and function (Seshadri et al., 2002; Wang and Cynader, 2001). In human, clinically abnormal level of Cys is associated with edema, liver damage, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and other health problems (Weerapana et al., 2010a, 2010b; Refsum et al., 2004). The detection of Cys both in academic research and in clinical applications is important. Thus, we can expect that the above-mentioned excellent properties of NRQDs may helps in selective and sensitive detection of Cys in living cells.

In this work, we present a facile approach to synthesize fluorescent NRQDs using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants by a mixed solvothermal method. The nitrogen content of the NRQDs is as high as 40.2%, which make it exhibit a good biocompatibility, water solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield. In cooperation with Cu2+, a nanoprobe for sensing of Cys based on the NRQDs was constructed. The coordination of NRQDs with Cu2+ result in the emission quenching of NRQDs, while the addition of Cys can lead to the fluorescence recovery. On the basis of the fluorescence change, we developed a facile specific nanoprobe for sensing of Cys in human serum and plasma with flexibility. Especially, owing to the unique properties of the NRQDs-Cu2+ complex-based nanoprobe with good membrane permeability and excellent biocompatibility, it was further applied to imaging of Cys in human lung adenocarcinoma cells with a high selectivity.

All the reagents and chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and were used as received. Human serum, plasma samples and A549 cells were provided by XiangYa School of Medicine, Central South University (Changsha, China). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-7000 Spectrofluorometer(Shimadzu, Japan). 1HNMR spectra were recorded with the Bruker AVB-400MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker biospin, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded with a PHILIPS TECNAI 10 TEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). Fluorescence imaging of A549 cells was examined by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM 710NLO, Germany). The pH measurements were performed with a pH-meter PB-10 (Sartorius, Germany).

Fluorescent NRQDs were synthesized by a mixed solvothermal method. As a required intermediate for the preparation of NRQDs, 2-azidoimidazole was prepared from 2-aminoimidazole, NaN3, and NaNO2 in aqueous HCl solution (Xue et al., 2015). Then the raw NRQDs were synthesized by a toluene/water-based solvethermal method using 2-azidoimidazole (1.10g), benzyltrimethyl ammonium bromide(0.001g), toluene(28mL) and aqueous ammonia (7mL, 25% in water) added into the high pressure reactor. The reaction mixture was warmed to 115°C and react for 2h. The resulting solution finally turned greenish black and even to dark brown, which implied the formation of NRQDs. After centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 25min, the supernatant was collected and then dialyzing against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane(7.0KD) for 36h. The dialysate was dried under vacuum-freeze to give an cinnamon-colored solid residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a fluorescent nanoprobe for Cys combined NRQDs with Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: An appropriate amount of NRQDs solution (10.0mL, 100μg/mL) and 2.5mL of Cu2+ (5.5 nmol/L) solution were added to a 20mL cuvette, then incubated for 10min for preparation the NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobe. Fluorescence quenching of NRQDs was used to monitor the formation of NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys with the NRQDs-Cu2+-based nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes and appropriate aliquot of Cys solution were transferred into a cuvette and incubated for 10min at room temperature. Then the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded at 472nm (Ex = 400nm), and the slit widths of emission and excitation were fixed at 5nm. The detection procedures for other interferences including amino acid and biothiols were similar to Cys. When real samples were detected, Cys standard solution was substituted by the human serum and plasma solutions. Each determination is repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular Cys fluorescence imaging in A549 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To examine whether the NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes can sense Cys in living cells, we then detected the Cys in A549 cells treated by the nanoprobes. Two days before fluorescence imaging, A549 cells were seeded in four plates containing sterile petridish and were incubated in a DMEM supplemented solution with the addition of 15% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (80U/mL), and streptomycin (80μg/mL) at room temperature in a 90% humidity atmosphere under 3% CO2 for two days. Prior to fluorescence imaging experiments, the cells were washed with a PBS buffer (20mmol, pH7.4) for three times and then incubated again with the NRQDs-Cu2+ probes (100μg/mL) at room temperature for 45min. Subsequently, the samples were washed with a PBS buffer (20mmol, pH7.4) three times to eliminate the remaining probes. For the negative and positive control samples, before incubation with the nanoprobes, the A549 cells were initially pretreated with a1.0mmol/L N-methylmaleimide solution (NMM, which is a thiol-reactive reagent for reducing the Cys level) or Cys (0.5mmol/L) at room temperature for 2h. After rinsing with a PBS buffer solution three times, the resulting A549 cells were further incubated with the probes at room temperature for 45min. After washing with the same buffer again, the cell images were then acquired.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the NRQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent NRQDs with high percentage of nitrogen atom were synthesized using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants by a mixed solvothermal method. As depicted in Scheme 1 a, the NRQDs were synthesized by a mixed solvothermal method using 2-azidoimidazole in a ammonia-toluene system in a pressure reactor, where the formation of nanoparticles, surface passivation and functionalization were accomplished simultaneously. The resulted NRQDs have high a percentage of nitrogen atom and exhibited high quantum yield, up to 34.7% (Table S1 in the Supplemental Information). Herein, the possible mechanism for the formation of NRQDs using 2-azidoimidozole as substrate is that ammonia water is a nucleophilic reagent in the ring-opening reaction of ethyleneimide, which also plays a pivotal role on passivating the active surface and give amino-groups on the surface of NRQDs (Ye et al., 2016). Conventional fabrication of amino-functionalized quantum dots require the process of nanoparticle synthesis, passivation and surface modification with nitrogen containing compounds. These nanostructures often change in grain structure, optical and electric properties due to the modification. Our approach integrates the formation of nanoparticles, surface passivation and functionalization, which is simpler and more efficient compared to conventional tedious and uncontrollable surface modification for the amino-functionalization of carbon quantum dots(CQDs).

As shown in SEM images in Fig. 1 , the NRQDs were well monodispersed and uniform with an average size of 5.5nm. The full scan XPS spectrum of NRQDs showed that C1s, N1s, and O1s signals appeared at 286.1, 399.3, and 531.9eV, respectively, and the XPS surveys of the NRQDs indicate a high nitrogen content, which is consistent to the results of their element analysis( Fig. 2 a). To confirm the N configurations in the NRQDs, N1s spectra were tested. The results indicate that the deconvoluted N1s XPS spectrum of NRQDs around 398.42eV, 398.75eV, 399.72eV and 400.89eV were pointed to the nitrogen atom of C˭N, NH2, C-N-C and N-C3, respectively(Fig. 2 b). FT-IR experiments were also carried out to confirm the presence of various functional groups in the NRQDs, which suggests characteristic absorption of O–H and N–H stretching vibration (Fig. 2 c). These NRQDs have nitrogen component up to 40.2%(Table S4 in the supporting information), which is much higher than the conventional CQDs modified with atoms reported previously (Xu et al., 2015) This result showed that the prepared NRQDs were abundant in amino moieties on the surfaces, which was also consistent by the corresponding FT-IR spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the nanoprobes and sensing principle for Cys detection
ce:section.ce:para: A nanoprobe based on the NRQDs combined with Cu2+ for sensitively and selectively sensing and imaging of Cys was built. The principle of the NRQDs-Cu2+ complex-based probes is illustrated in Scheme 1 b. This nanoprobe is a kind of nanocomposites, where the NRQDs serve as fluorescence reporters and Cu2+ functions as fluorescence controllers. In the sensing diads, the amino groups of NRQDs coordinate with Cu2+, resulting in efficient quenching of their fluorescence via energy transfer from NRQDs to Cu2+. The strong affinity between Cu 2+ and Cys leads to the formation of Cu2+-Cys complexes and cause the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of NRQDs, thus the fluorescence of NRQDs recover. Our proposed principle of NRQDs-Cu2+ complex-based probes for sensing Cys has been proved by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NRQDs were recorded. As shown in Figure S1, a in the Supplemental Information, NRQDs aqueous solution has one obvious UV absorbance bands at 312nm, which can be assigned to abundant –C˭N− bond and π–π* transition within NRQDs. The NRQDs aqueous solution was light brown, transparent, and clear under sunlight, and exhibited bright blue-green luminescence under UV excitation at 365nm (Figure S1, b in the Supplemental Information), indicating that the NRQDs had a good fluorescence property. The fluorescence spectra of NRQDs, NRQDs-Cu2+complexes and NRQDs-Cu2+ reacted with Cys indicate that Cys can cause significant changes in fluorescence of NRQDs-Cu2+, which is taken as the basis for Cys sensing(Figure S1, c in the Supplemental Information). The fluorescence spectra of NRQDs show a typical excitation-dependent feature as depicted in Figure S1, d in the Supplemental Information; the fluorescence peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength(Excitation maximum:380nm; Emission maximum: 472nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the specific nanoprobes for Cys
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain a better response to the fluorescence nanoprobes, we optimized the conditions, including the volume ratio of NRQDs to Cu2+, reacting time, recovery time and pH value. The results showed that the volume ratio of NRQDs (4.0mL, 80μg/mL) to Cu2+ (1.0mL, 2.0 nmol/L) was 4:1 when the fluorescence of NRQDs was quenched completely(Figure S2, a in the Supporting Information). Both fluorescence quenching and recovery were completed within 3min(Figure S2, b in the Supporting Information). Due to the effects of acidity on the detecting Cys, therefore we investigated the effect of pH value from 3.0 to 11.0 on the fluorescence of the nanoprobes. The emission intensity of the NRQDs-Cu2+ complex-based nanoprobe showed only a slight decrease as the pH value changed (Figure S2, c in the Supporting Information). Therefore, pH 7.4 was used as the pH value to test the response of the nanoprobes based on the comprehensive consideration.

For an excellent fluorescent nanoprobe, high selectivity is a matter of necessity. The fluorescence response of NRQDs-based nanoprobe to various amino acids (Phe, Ala, Gly, Glu, Arg, Lys, Tys, Leu, Ser, Val, Thr, Asn and Leu) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with that of NRQDs-based probes reacted with Cys, no obvious changes in fluorescence were observed upon addition of the other amino acids, denoting that the formation of NRQDs-Cu2+chelates is a key for the selective recognition of Cys. Furthermore, we examined the response of the nanoprobes to Cys in the presence of other species, and most of the other species only displayed minimum interference (Fig. 3 b). These results indicated a high selectivity of the NRQDs-Cu2+-based probes in sensing Cys. The interferences in the serum and plasma were below the value of the tolerable, which demonstrate that the proposed method has a high selectivity for the detection of Cys in complex biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in human serum and plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate practicality of this method, the Cys concentrations in human serum and plasma have been determined fluorometrically with the NRQDs-Cu2+-based nanoprobes. Under the optimized conditions, there is a good linear relationship between the emission intensity and the concentration of Cys. As shown in Fig. 4, the linear range was obtained from 0.15 μmol/L to 10.5 μmol/L (R2=0.9967), with a 0.03 μmol/L detection limit (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was lower than the reported level of Cys (Quach et al., 2011; Dai et al., 2015; Hong et al., 2013). The capability of the NRQDs-Cu2+-based nanoprobe was also evaluated by the detection of Cys in human serum and plasma (Table S5 in the Spplemental Information). The recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 97.2% to 105.7%, indicating the practicability of the NRQDs-based sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of Cys in A549 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the application of the proposed probes, the NRQDs-Cu2+ complex–based probes for intracellular Cys imaging in A549 cells were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, blue fluorescence was found in the incubated cells in 0.5μM NRQDs both with(B) and without(C) pretreatment with 1.0mM NMM, which attribute to the emission of NRQDs. These indicated that the NRQDs have good cell membrane permeability, and make fast detection of the intracellular Cys. On the other hand, to validate that the NRQDs-Cu2+ nanoprobes is able to respond to changes of the intracellular Cys level in living cells, we pretreated the A549 cells with a 1.0mM NMM solution and then incubated in a 0.5 μmol/L NRQDs-Cu2+solution. Almost no fluorescence was found in the pretreated A549 cells(Fig. 5 F); while the cells incubated in a NRQDs-Cu2+solution without pretreatment with NMM exhibited strong fluorescence in the blue channel(Fig. 5 G). These results successfully demonstrate that the proposed nanoprobes NRQDs-Cu2+ is capable of imaging Cys and discriminating different concentration levels of intracellular Cys in living cells.

In summary, a facile and quick “synthesis-modification integration” methodology for the preparation of fluorescent NRQDs by mixed solvothermal method was developed using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants. These NRQDs have high percentage of the nitrogen atoms up to 40.2%, endowed them with tunable fluorescent emission, bright luminescence and favourable biocompatibility. Furthermore, we rationally constructed a novel probe for Cys based on the coordination of NRQDs with Cu2+. The proposed nanoprobe enables facile rapid and robust Cys sensing in human serum and plasma with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. This strategy for the synthesis of CQDs may give a new sight for the preparation of high nitrogen component nanomaterials and broadening analytical application of NRQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11405013)
